text: A Triaxial Accelerometer Based on Differential Resonant Beams and Force-Balanced Capacitive Plates
Jianqiang Han, Yongsheng Zhu, Gonghao Tao, Zhengqian Zhao, Yijun Yin, Wenju Niu, and Lizhen Dong

Abstract—This paper presents the design, simulation, fabrication, and experiment of a triaxial accelerometer based on differential resonant beams and force-balanced capacitive plates. The triaxial accelerometer is composed of a proof mass, four bridge resonators with both end clamped and operating in flexure vibration mode, four L-type supporting beams, and a top plate and a bottom plate. The in-plane acceleration is measured by bridge resonators. The gravity center of the proof mass and the neutral layer of L-type supporting beams are located within the same plane to minimize the rotation of the proof mass subjected to in-plane acceleration. The cross-axis sensitivities of X-axis and Y-axis resonant beams under lateral in-plane acceleration are $1.97\%$ and $1.71\%$ , respectively. A force-balanced differential plate capacitor composed of a proof mass, a top plate, and a bottom plate is used to detect acceleration along Z-axis. The small displacement of the proof mass in the normal direction of the chip reduces the cross-axis sensitivity of the bridge resonators subjected to acceleration along Z-axis. The complementation of the resonance detection mechanism and the force balance capacitance mode makes the triaxial accelerometer exhibit low cross-axis sensitivity. The simulated and experimental results prove this conclusion.
Index Terms—Resonant accelerometer, force-balanced capacitive accelerometer, triaxial accelerometer, cross-axis.
I. INTRODUCTION

MICRO-accelerometers have the advantages of light weight, small volume, low power consumption and cost, easy integration and mass production. With regard to detection mechanism, there are piezoresistive [1]–[5], capacitive [6]–[15], piezoelectric [16]–[18], convective [19], [20], tunneling [21]–[23] and resonant accelerometers [24]–[36]. Regarding on the numbers of sensitive axis, there are uniaxial, dual-axis and triaxial accelerometers. The triaxial accelerometers are required for sophisticated motion control systems in wide industrial fields such as the robotics and automotive industries, and civil fields such as the smart phone,

games controllers and the pedometer [37]. Early triaxial accelerometers were only the combinations of three uniaxial accelerometers placed orthogonally. The accelerometers have the disadvantages of difficult assembly, large size, high cost, and so on. At present, most of reported and commercial triaxial accelerometers are monolithically integrated. These accelerometers have the advantages of high accuracy of position between axes, low cost, small volume, and so on. According to the number of proof masses, the monolithic integrated triaxial accelerometers can be divided into multiple proof-mass accelerometers and single proof-mass accelerometers. Triaxial accelerometers with multiple proof mass detect acceleration along X, Y and Z-axis through two or three piezoresistive or capacitive sensing units integrated on a sensor chip [5]-[9]. These accelerometers show low cross-axis sensitivity because the structures of different detection unit can be optimized independently. However, these accelerometers generally exhibit a large chip area, such as $11.8 \times 4.8 \mathrm{~mm}^2$ reported in literature [6], [7], $12 \times 7 \mathrm{~mm}^2$ in literature [9]. The process compatibility among different detecting units is a key issue should be considered.

Triaxial accelerometers with single proof mass exhibiting small chip area have been a research topic and development trend. According to the mechanism, accelerometers with single proof-mass can be divided into piezoresistive, capacitive and convective accelerometers. Piezoresistive triaxial accelerometers use PMOS transistors or piezoresistors to detect acceleration along X, Y, Z-axis [1]-[4]. The piezoresistive accelerometers have the advantages of mature fabrication technology, simple interface circuit, low output impedance and easy to realize high-g sensors. However, triaxial piezoresistive accelerometers show the disadvantages of complex detection mechanism and resistor arrangement, large temperature drift, difficult to meet high performance applications.

Capacitive triaxial accelerometers are the most widely reported triaxial accelerometers [10]–[15]. Their sensing structures include comb-finger capacitors fabricated by surface micromachining technology and flat-plate capacitors fabricated by bulk micromachining technology. The capacitive accelerometer working in open-loop mode has narrow bandwidth, worse linearity and poor dynamic range. An effective solution to this problem is adopting a closed-loop servo system, which applies a feedback force to keep movable plate in the equilibrium position [11]–[15]. Acceleration can be

measured by detecting the feedback voltage. The accelerometers based on the force-balanced mechanism is characterized by good linearity, large dynamic range, small influence of processing error on sensor performances, small temperature coefficients of sensitivity, bandwidth and drift.

The convective triaxial accelerometer consists of a sealed air chamber, a heater and several temperature sensors [19], [20]. It has the advantages of simple fabrication techniques, high compatibility with CMOS process, strong shock-resistance and high reliability. But the accelerometer has the disadvantages of low resolution, slow response and narrow dynamic range, so it is not suitable for high-precision detection.

Different detection mechanisms have their own advantages and disadvantages. Piezoresistive microbeams, torsional capacitors, plate capacitors and resonating beams are suitable for detecting acceleration along Z-axis. Comb-finger capacitor and double-ended tuning fork (DETF) are suitable for detecting in-plane acceleration along X-axis or Y-axis. This paper intends to design a triaxial accelerometer with single proof mass of full-wafer thickness. The in-plane acceleration along X-axis and Y-axis are measured by thin bridge resonators with both end clamped and operating in flexure vibration mode. A differential plate capacitor working in force-balanced mode is used to detect acceleration along Z-axis. The small displacement of the proof mass in the normal direction of the sensor chip reduces the cross-axis sensitivity of bridge resonators subjected to acceleration along Z-axis.
II. THE STRUCTURE AND FABRICATION OF TRIAXIAL ACCELEROMETERS

Previous literatures have reported many manufacturing processes for in-plane resonant accelerometers, such as surface micromachining [24], [25], mixed micromachining [26], SOI technology [27]–[31], deep dry silicon on glass (DDSOG) process [32]. These accelerometers are composed of three parts: a proof mass, microleverages and resonators. The proof mass converts acceleration into inertia force, then the microleverage amplifies the inertia force, and finally thin resonant beams or DETF resonators convert the inertia force into resonance frequency. However, thin and complex structure of microleverage and DETF would decrease the stability and yield. Bulk micromechanical resonant accelerometers used to sense acceleration along Z-axis [33]–[36] feature a high sensitivity because proof mass with full-wafer thickness can be fabricated. However, the proof mass with full-wafer thickness cannot be supported with DETFs. The stiffness of the bridges usually supporting the proof mass for detecting acceleration along Z-axis is too large for detecting in-plane acceleration. How to support the proof mass with full-wafer thickness is a key problem in developing bulk micromechanical in-plane resonant accelerometers.

In this paper, a novel triaxial accelerometer based on differential resonant beams and force-balanced capacitive plates is presented. The triaxial accelerometer is schematically shown in Fig. 1. It comprises a top plate, a bottom plate and a sensor chip. The sensor chip consists of a proof mass supported by four L-type supporting beams and four thin bridge resonators.

image_caption: Fig. 1. (a) Scheme of the triaxial accelerometer, (b) the backside of the top plate, (c) the backside of the bottom plate.
Fig. 2. The process flow of the top plates and bottom plates.

text: The gravity center of the proof mass and the neutral layer of L-type supporting beams are located within a same plane to minimize the rotation of the proof mass subjected to in-plane acceleration. The axial strain of bridge resonators is mainly determined by the in-plane stiffness of supporting beams. Thin bridge resonators are located at the upper surface of the sensor chip in order to photolithograph resistors and pattern metal lines. The design, simulation, fabrication and experiment of differential resonant beams have been reported previously [38]. This paper only briefly introduces its structure and working principle.

A force-balanced differential plate capacitor composed of a proof mass, a top plate and a bottom plate is used to detect acceleration along Z-axis. Fixed upper electrode and lower electrode are fabricated on the top plate and the bottom plate, respectively. The gap between the fixed electrodes and movable proof mass is $5\mu \mathrm{m}$ . Blunt silicon tips with a height of $2.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ stand on the surface of fixed electrodes prevent the proof mass being attached with fixed electrodes.
Fig. 2 (1) - (6) show the fabrication process of the top plate along the A-A' section and the bottom plate along the

image_caption: Fig. 3. The SEM photograph of the sensor chip.

text: B-B' section in Fig. 1. The fabrication process of the top plate starts with n-type (100) silicon wafers of $389\mu \mathrm{m}$ thick. Thermal oxide layer of $0.6\mu \mathrm{m}$ is grown on both sides of wafers at $1100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . Apart from bonding area and eight square masks for etching bumps, the silicon oxide film at the backside of wafers is patterned and removed in BOE solution. The exposed silicon is etched to a depth of $5\mu \mathrm{m}$ in $40\%$ KOH solution at $60^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the bumps with a height of $2.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ are formed at the same time to prevent the mass from adhering to the upper electrode. Silicon dioxide is grown again on the surface of silicon as shown in Fig. 2(1). The silicon dioxide film in the center of the chip and the middle of four margins at the front side of wafers are patterned for etching a through hole and exposing pads on the main chip, respectively. The exposed silicon is etched to a depth of $200\mu \mathrm{m}$ in $40\%$ KOH solution at $80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . Then the silicon oxide film at the backside of wafers is patterned to etch silicon providing space for bridge resonators vibrating and reduce squeeze-film damping, shown in Fig. 2(2). The silicon wafer is etched again in KOH solution until the holes at the center of chips penetrate the back of silicon wafers. At last, Ti/Au film of $240~\mathrm{nm}$ and Al film of $1\mu \mathrm{m}$ are sputtered on the electrode of backside of the wafer and through hole, respectively. The wafer is annealed at $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for 30 minutes to strengthen the adhesion between the metal film and silicon oxide film, shown in Fig. 2(3).

The fabrication flow of bumps on the bottom plate is the same as that on the top plate, as is shown in Fig. 2(4). The mask of through hole at the backside of the wafer is wet etched in BOE solution and the silicon is anisotropic etched from the backside of the wafer to form through hole. Shown in Fig. 2(5). Then Ti/Au films are sputtered on the four corners of the front side, the backside and the through holes. Finally, The wafer is annealed at $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for 30 minutes, shown in Fig. 2(6). The SEM photograph of the sensor chip is shown in Fig. 3.

The amorphous silicon film on the four corners of the front-side and the backside of the sensor chip is eutectically bonded with Au film at the bottom surface of top plate and the upper surface of bottom plate, respectively. The backside of the bottom plate is eutectically bonded on the gold-plated installation area of ceramic tube shell with an Au-Sn solder with the thickness of $50~\mu \mathrm{m}$ . The pads on the chip are connected to the terminals of the package by aluminum wires. The geometry of the triaxial accelerometer is listed in Table I. The final

text: B. The Sensitivity of the Plate Capacitor for Detecting Acceleration Along Z-Axis

Fig. 4 shows the model of the differential plate capacitor for detecting acceleration along Z-axis. In force-balanced mode, an AC voltage $(\pm u(t))$ superimposed on a DC bias voltage $(\pm U_{d})$ is applied to fixed plates in order to detect the variation of capacitance. The feedback voltage $(U_{f})$ is applied to the movable plate. The electrostatic force applied to the proof mass is
Here $A$ is the opposing area between the movable electrode and fixed electrodes. $d$ is the initial distance between electrodes. $z$ is the displacement of the proof mass.
When the differential plate capacitor works in force-balanced mode, $z << d$ , the electrostatic force is approximately equal to

The electrostatic force $(F_{e})$ includes two terms, the first term is the electrostatic force generated by the bias voltage $(U_{d})$ . The second term is the electrostatic force generated by the high frequency AC voltage $(u(t))$ . Since the frequency of AC voltage is 2 orders of magnitude higher than the first-order resonance frequency of the accelerometer, the displacement of the movable plate caused by the high frequency electrostatic force is very small and can be reasonably neglected [43]. Thus, the electrostatic force is approximately equal to

In force-balanced mode, the proof mass tends to be at equilibrium position under the action of electrostatic force, that is, $z \approx 0$ , as a result, the elastic force is also approximately 0. The inertia force is balanced by the electrostatic force, that is, $F_{e} = ma_{z}$ . Therefore, the feedback voltage $(U_{f})$ can be calculated as [44]
The sensitivity of the force-balanced plate capacitor is

image_caption: Fig. 5. The vertical displacement of the proof mass under different acceleration along Z-axis.

text: As can be seen from equation (10), the sensitivity of the plate capacitor for detecting acceleration along Z-axis can be improved significantly with increasing mass $m$ . Therefore, the differential plate capacitor with proof mass of full-wafer thickness can exhibit high sensitivity. For the fabricated triaxial accelerometer, the mass of proof, the opposing area of electrodes and distance between the movable electrode and fixed electrodes is $1.56 \times 10^{-6}$ kg, $1170 \mu \mathrm{m} \times 1170 \mu \mathrm{m}$ and $5 \mu \mathrm{m}$ respectively. The theoretical sensitivity for detecting Z-axis acceleration can be calculated as $-3.496 \mathrm{~V/g}$ by equation (10) under a DC bias voltage $(\pm U_d)$ of $4.5 \mathrm{~V}$ .
C. The Measurement Range of the Plate Capacitor for Detecting Acceleration Along Z-Axis

The measurement range of bridge resonators has been described in detail in the previous literature [38], only the measurement range of the plate capacitor for detecting acceleration along Z-axis is discussed here. In open-loop mode, the simulated result shows that the maximum stress occurs at the clamped ends of L-type supporting beams when acceleration along Z-axis is input. The maximum stress at the clamped ends is $34\mathrm{MPa}$ under $100\mathrm{g}$ acceleration along Z-axis, The stress is less than the bending strength of the monocrystalline silicon. Meanwhile the maximum strain at the Wheatstone bridges on both X-axis and Y-axis bridge resonators is $85~\mu \varepsilon$ Bumps located on the surface of fixed electrodes limit the movement of the proof mass within half of the original gap from the proof mass to the bottom plate. As can be seen from Fig. 5, the displacement of the proof mass reaches the limit value of $2.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ when $65\mathrm{g}$ acceleration along Z-axis is input. Therefore the input acceleration along Z-axis should be less than $65\mathrm{g}$ in the open loop mode if the displacement of the proof mass is limited to half of the initial gap from the proof mass to the bottom plate.

In the closed-loop mode, the feedback voltage $(U_{f})$ is proportional to acceleration along $Z$ -axis $(a_{z})$ as can be seen from equation (9). The proportionality coefficient is related to the bias voltage $U_{d}$ applied to the capacitor and the geometry of the accelerometer. Since the feedback voltage $U_{f}$ can only be changed between $[-U_{d}, + U_{d}]$ , the maximum detectable

As can be seen from equation (11), the maximum detectable Z-axis acceleration of the differential plate capacitor will increase with decreasing mass $m$ . However, decreasing the mass of the proof will reduce the sensitivity at the same time. The theoretical detectable acceleration of the fabricated differential plate capacitor in force-balanced mode can be calculated as $1.28\mathrm{g}$ under a DC bias voltage $(\pm U_{d})$ of $4.5\mathrm{V}$ .
IV. CROSS-AXIS SENSITIVITY
A. The Cross-Axis Sensitivity of Bridge Resonators Subjected to Lateral In-Plane Acceleration

The cross-axis sensitivity, defined as the ratio of the output of an accelerometer subjected to lateral acceleration to that of the accelerometer subjected to primary axis, is an important factor for precise applications requiring high accuracy. The cross-axis sensitivity of X-axis bridge resonators subjected to the acceleration along Y-axis is

Here $S_{XX}$ is the sensitivity of X-axis bridge resonators subjected to the acceleration along X-axis, $S_{XY}$ is the sensitivity of X-axis bridge resonators subjected to the acceleration along Y-axis.

The simulated resonant frequency shift of X-axis bridge resonators subjected to $1\mathrm{g}$ acceleration along X-axis and Y-axis is $25.5\mathrm{Hz}$ and $0.38\mathrm{Hz}$ , respectively. So $\delta_{XY}$ is calculated to be $1.5\%$ . The simulated resonant frequency shift of Y-axis bridge resonators subjected to $1\mathrm{g}$ acceleration along Y-axis and X-axis is $15.3\mathrm{Hz}$ and $0.14\mathrm{Hz}$ , respectively. So $\delta_{YX}$ is calculated to be $0.9\%$ .
B. The Cross-Axis Sensitivity of Bridge Resonators Subjected to the Acceleration Along Z-Axis

In the open-loop mode, the simulated resonant frequency shift of X-axis and Y-axis bridge resonators is $0.44\mathrm{Hz}$ subjected to $1\mathrm{g}$ acceleration along Z-axis. So the sensitivity of X-axis and Y-axis bridge resonators subjected to the acceleration along Z-axis is $S_{XZ} = S_{YZ} = 0.44\mathrm{Hz / g}$ , the cross-axis sensitivity $(\delta_{XZ},\delta_{YZ})$ subjected to the acceleration along Z-axis is calculated to be $1.7\%$ $2.8\%$ respectively.

In the closed-loop force-balanced mode, the proof mass will remain in the equilibrium position due to the feedback voltage. The performances of the sensor system are determined by the feedback loop circuit rather than by the mechanical structure, which is difficult to control. The proof mass in force-balanced mode can be considered to be in the equilibrium position as long as the acceleration does not exceed the feedback force control range. The theoretical and measured cross-axis sensitivity of the bridge resonators subjected to the acceleration along Z-axis is approximately zero.

image_caption: Fig. 6. Frequency shifts of X-axis resonant beams subjected to different acceleration along X-axis.

text: C. The Cross-Axis Sensitivity of the Z-Axis Capacitor Under In-Plane Acceleration

Since the area of movable electrodes is slightly smaller than area of fixed electrodes, respectively, the minor translation of the proof mass subjected to in-plane acceleration will neither change the distance between the movable electrode and fixed electrodes nor change the opposite area of the electrodes.

Whether in open-loop mode or force-balanced mode, the proof mass will rotate a very small angle around Y axis when the acceleration along X-axis is input although the gravity center of the proof mass lies within the neutral plane of L-type supporting beams. However, the differential capacitance will not be changed. The cross-axis sensitivity of the differential capacitor subjected to the acceleration along X-axis can be ignored reasonably. Similarly, the cross-axis sensitivity of the differential capacitor subjected to the acceleration along Y-axis can also be ignored.
V. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
A. The Sensitivity of Bridge Resonators

To drive the bridge resonator to self-oscillate, a closed-loop self-exciting circuit based on phase-locked loop is designed and reported in previous literature [45]. A Agilent 34401A $6\frac{1}{2}$ digit multi-meter is used to monitor the output resonant frequency of the closed-loop circuit. A frequency counter based on field-programmable gate array (FPGA) is used to measure the resonant frequency. USB communication line is used for FPGA communication with computer and data can be received through serial debugging assistant.

The sensitivity of the developed accelerometer is measured with a rotary indexing table. The table placed parallel to gravitational acceleration is used to simulate the axial acceleration in a range from $-1\mathrm{g}$ to $+1\mathrm{g}$ . The sensitivity of the accelerometer is measured by rotating the rotary indexing table and fitted by the least squares method. The measured resonant frequency shifts of a X-axis bridge resonator under different X-axis acceleration and a Y-axis bridge resonator under different Y-axis acceleration are shown in Figures 6 and 7, respectively. The sensitivity of the X-axis bridge resonator $(S_X)$ and

text: Y-axis bridge resonator $(S_{Y})$ is $21.30\mathrm{Hz / g}$ and $12.24\mathrm{Hz / g}$ , respectively.

The measured sensitivity is less than theoretical sensitivity of $37.5\mathrm{Hz / g}$ for detecting the acceleration along X-axis determined by equation (4) and $35.0\mathrm{Hz / g}$ for detecting the acceleration along Y-axis determined by equation (5), but close to the simulated sensitivity of $25.5\mathrm{Hz / g}$ for detecting the acceleration along X-axis and $15.3\mathrm{Hz / g}$ for detecting the acceleration along Y-axis. This is mainly because thinner bridge resonators located at the upper surface of the sensor chip increase the asymmetry of the mechanical structure and cause a slight rotation of the proof mass under in-plane acceleration.
B. The Cross-Axis Sensitivity of Bridge Resonators Subjected to Lateral In-Plane Acceleration

Although the bridge resonators exhibit theoretically low cross sensitivity to lateral in-plane acceleration because the gravity center of the proof mass lies within the neutral plane of L-type supporting beams, it is still necessary to measure the actual value. The measured resonant frequency of the above-mentioned X-axis bridge resonator changes from $63990.48\mathrm{Hz}$ to $63990.06\mathrm{Hz}$ when the acceleration in the Y-axis direction changes from $0\mathrm{g}$ to $1\mathrm{g}$ . The lateral sensitivity $(S_{XY})$ of the X-axis bridge resonator subjected to the acceleration along Y-axis is calculated as $0.42\mathrm{Hz / g}$ . As a result, the cross-axis sensitivity $(\delta_{XY})$ of the X-axis bridge resonator subjected to the acceleration along Y-axis, the ratio of $S_{XY}$ to $S_{XX}$ , is calculated to be $1.97\%$ . Similarly, the lateral sensitivity $(S_{YX})$ of the Y-axis bridge resonator subjected to the acceleration along X-axis is measured to be $0.21\mathrm{Hz / g}$ . The cross-axis sensitivity $(\delta_{YX})$ of the Y-axis bridge resonator subjected to the acceleration along X-axis can be calculated as $1.71\%$ .
C. The Sensitivity of the Differential Plate Capacitor

The scheme of the closed loop circuit for measuring acceleration along Z-axis is shown in Fig. 8. A DC bias voltage $(\pm U_{d})$ of $4.5\mathrm{V}$ passing through two low-pass filters consisting of $R_{1}$ , $C_{3}$ and $R_{2}$ , $C_{5}$ are applied to the top and bottom

image_caption: Fig. 8. The scheme of the closed loop circuit for measuring acceleration along Z-axis.
Fig. 9. The measurement setup for measuring acceleration.

text: plate, respectively. Two square wave signals $(\pm U_{s})$ with an amplitude of $2.25\mathrm{V}$ output from a MS3110 chip passing through two high-pass filters consisting of $C_3$ , $R_1$ and $C_5$ , $R_2$ are used as modulate signals. The square waves superposed on DC bias voltage $(\pm U_d)$ are applied to the top and bottom plate, respectively. The feedback voltage $(U_f)$ is applied to the movable proof mass.

The measurement setup for measuring Z-axis acceleration is shown in Figure 9. The gravity field reversal method is used to test the feedback voltage of the servo-controlled differential plate capacitor. The amplification factor of the differential amplifier and force feedback generator are set to 5 and 0.9, respectively. The proportional control coefficient $K_{\mathrm{p}}$ and integral control coefficient $K_{\mathrm{I}}$ of PI controller are set to 5 and 10. The feedback voltage subjected to $1\mathrm{g}$ and $-1\mathrm{g}$ acceleration along Z-axis is $-3.487\mathrm{V}$ and $3.195\mathrm{V}$ , respectively. Correspondingly, the sensitivity $(S_Z)$ of the closed-loop detection system is $-3.341\mathrm{V/g}$ . It is slightly less than the theoretical value of $-3.496\mathrm{V/g}$ . The slight deviation between theoretical and measured sensitivity originates from the deviation between the parameters $(m,d)$ used in theoretical calculation and that of fabricated device.
VI. CONCLUSION

This paper presents a prototype of triaxial accelerometer with single proof mass to sense X, Y and Z-axis acceleration. The in-plane acceleration along X-axis and Y-axis are detected by flexural bridge resonators. A differential plate capacitor operated in force-balanced mode is used to detect Z-axis acceleration. Since the gravity center of the proof mass and
HAN et al.: TRIAXIAL ACCELEROMETER BASED ON DIFFERENTIAL RESONANT BEAMS AND FORCE-BALANCED CAPACITIVE PLATES
Authorized licensed use limited to: KU Leuven University Library. Downloaded on January 19,2026 at 17:49:06 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.

the neutral layer of L-type supporting beams are located on a same plane, the rotation of the proof mass subjected to in-plane acceleration can be reduced. The small displacement of the proof mass in Z-axis reduces the cross-axis sensitivity of the bridge resonators subjected to the acceleration along Z-axis. Three detection units can be optimized independently. The complementation of resonance detection mechanism and the force balance capacitance mode makes the triaxial accelerometer to exhibit low cross-axis sensitivity. The simulated and experimental results prove this conclusion.

text: Zhengqian Zhao received the bachelor's degree from Xinxiang University in 2017. He is currently pursuing the master's degree with the College of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering, China Jiliang University.
His research interests are in the areas of resonant-force accelerometer and simulation of performance.
Jianqiang Han received the Ph.D. degree in electron science and technology from Xi'an Jiaotong University in 2003.

He was a Post-Doctoral Fellow with State Key Lab of Transducer Technology, Shanghai Institute of Microsystem and Information Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, from 2004 to 2005. Since 2006, he has been an Associate Professor and then a Professor with the College of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering, China Jiliang University. His research interests are in the areas of micromachin
ing resonant accelerometers, resonant IR detectors, and thin-film thermal converter.
Yijun Yin received the master's degree in mechanical and electrical engineering from China Jiliang University in 2017.
He is currently with Hangzhou Focused Photonics Inc. His research interests are in the areas of the three-axis micro acceleration sensor package based on the resonance - force balance capacitive.

Yongsheng Zhu received the bachelor's degree from West Anhui University in 2017. He is currently pursuing the master's degree with the College of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering, China Jiliang University.
His research interests are in the areas of high-precise voltage/ammeter based on resonant thin film thermoelectric converter.

Wenju Niu received the bachelor's degree from Anyang Institute of Technology in 2015. He is currently pursuing the master's degree with the College of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering, China Jiliang University.
His research interests are in the areas of the three axis acceleration sensor based on the mechanism of composite detection of resonant-tunneling force balance.

Gonghao Tao received the bachelor's degree from Anhui Polytechnic University in 2017. He is currently pursuing the master's degree with the College of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering, China Jiliang University.
His research interests are in the areas of study on metal thin film materials based on magnetron sputtering.

Lizhen Dong received the bachelor's degree from Shandong Agricultural University in 2016. She is currently pursuing the master's degree with the College of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering, China Jiliang University.
Her research interests are in the areas of closed-loop self-excited circuit based on electrostatic excitation/capacitance detection micro-bridge resonator.
HAN et al.: TRIAXIAL ACCELEROMETER BASED ON DIFFERENTIAL RESONANT BEAMS AND FORCE-BALANCED CAPACITIVE PLATES
